Careful though, the triplet:
```python
condition and val_on_true or val_on_false
```
Has some non-intuitive results:
```python
In [17]: condition=True; val_on_true=''; val_on_false='fallback'

In [18]: condition and val_on_true or val_on_false
Out[18]: 'fallback'
```

that's why to be in the safe side, every time you have a condition and two results (triplet) you should better use the `val_on_true if condition else val_on_false` construction. With two values that does not happen, but just keep it in mind.

In general I don't like a lot class methods. From the functionality point of view, this makes sense because it's great having something we can use to duplicate a context creating a kind of "clone". From the API point of view, we already have the context (because you're passing the `ctx`) so it's better if it's an instance method.

This results in:
```python
new_ctx = old_ctx.clone()
```

Now we can talk about the method name, because `clone()` is fine but it's not a real clone (we only copy some attributes). Since I don't have better ideas at the moment, we may use `clone()` and add in the docstring a better description saying: "This method doesn't clone registered spans for that `Context`".

I have taken your advice, and in doing so converted the interface to be two abstract methods: `get_compiler(self)` and `compile(self, compile_request)`, and moving much of the logic in both `NativeTask` as well as `C/CppCompile` into `NativeCompile` instead (including generating the compile request). This also makes the `execute(self)` method in `NativeCompile` much easier to follow.

To move the `strict_deps` logic out of `NativeTask`, I introduced a separate `NativeTargetDependencies` product to cache the target's dependencies, which the `LinkSharedLibraries` task consumes (with a note that it should probably store something besides just the target).

I also moved all the options to the new `C/CppCompileSettings` subsystems, which took a lot of unnecessary complexity out of the tasks as well.

In DBN the cardinality of the node will be same at every time slice. Now, If the node queried  using `get_cardinality` is in time slice = 0 then `temp_node = node`. However, if the queried node is not in time slice 0 then using `1-node[1]` ,i brought it to time slice 0 and then proceed to output the cardinality of the queired node at time slice =0. 

For example - 
If `dbn.get_cardinality(('D',1))` is called then -
`node = ('D',1)`
`node[1] = 1`
therefore, `temp_node = (node[0],1 - node[1]) = ('D',0)` 

Similarly if   `dbn.get_cardinality(('D',0))` is called then -
`node = ('D',0)`
`node[1] = 0`
therefore, `temp_node = node = ('D',0)`.

Never raise `Exception`. It's a Bad Thing™ to do. If a client wants to catch this exception, they will write something like this:

```python
try:
    Task.wait_for_task()
except Exception:
```

I'm not that worried about this, we should almost certainly leave this for now, and I know we do the same thing in Apache to work around the same problems, BUT from the documentation for [\_\_hash\_\_](https://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#object.__hash__):

> If a class defines mutable objects and implements an \_\_eq\_\_() method, it should not implement \_\_hash\_\_(), since the implementation of hashable collections requires that a key’s hash value is immutable (if the object’s hash value changes, it will be in the wrong hash bucket).

After thinking about this more, I don't think we should ask for this. I think we should restrict issues on this repo to be issues with Certbot and not more general problems configuring their server. I've found the domain is really useful for helping people debug a webserver they manually misconfigured with certificates from Certbot, but almost never needed to debug a problem with Certbot itself. I asked about this in IRC and everyone who responded agreed.

This coupled with the fact that people are very hesitant to give up their domain makes me think we should nix this question entirely.

This is definitely a nit, but it might be kind of nice to:

1. Have a separate test class for `ExitHandler` to simplify the classes and make it clear what functionality is tested where.
2. Ensure all of the other functionality of `ExitHandler` works now and in the future.

Doing this might be a bit tricky due to the way tests are automatically found and run, but one way would be to:

1. Make a `ExitHandlerTest` class that inherits from this class.
2. In `ExitHandlerTest`, set `self.handler` to an `ExitHandler`.
3. Change the name of the methods you're adding to something like `test_regular_exit` and override this in `ExitHandler`.

This is only a suggestion so feel free to push back and/or ignore it, but I think it should be pretty easy and might be worth it.
